currentUse: Site redeveloped: seven affordable owner-occupied homes (three duplexes and one single-family house) built by nonprofit Bread & Roses Housing on the former church lot on Washington Street, completed by June 2021
summary: The "unnamed" Lithuanian church of Haverhill can now be identified: it was St. George's, the Lithuanian parish on Washington Street in the Mount Washington neighborhood, one of four ethnic parishes (with Polish St. Michael's, Italian St. Rita's, and French St. Joseph's) that the Archdiocese of Boston consolidated into All Saints Parish in 1998. The 1892-built brick church, acquired by Lithuanians around 1910, then sat unoccupied for two decades — briefly serving as a police precinct before falling derelict, its stained glass still visible through broken windows in 2017. In October 2018 the Haverhill City Council unanimously granted Lawrence-based nonprofit Bread & Roses Housing a special permit to demolish it for affordable owner-occupied townhouses, and the 127-year-old building was torn down in mid-November 2019, with a former parishioner salvaging bricks as mementos of the church where she was married and her children baptized. The plan was scaled back from ten units to seven, and in June 2021 Governor Charlie Baker and Lt. Governor Karyn Polito visited the site to celebrate the seven completed affordable homes. No trace of the church remains on site, and no Lithuanian preservation effort, memorial, or reunion activity connected to the parish was found in recent press.

developments.headline: City Council approves demolition-for-housing plan
developments.detail: Haverhill City Council unanimously granted Bread & Roses Housing, a Lawrence-based nonprofit, a special permit to demolish the former St. George Catholic Church — vacant for 20 years — and replace it with 10 affordable, owner-occupied townhouses in two buildings, at an estimated cost of $2.5–3 million.

sources.title: Church to be replaced with affordable homes

developments.headline: Housing plan scaled back; demolition imminent
developments.detail: Bread & Roses Housing revised its plan for the former St. George's property in the Mount Washington neighborhood to fewer units than originally proposed, with demolition of the church expected to begin soon.

sources.title: Old St. George Church to be demolished for housing

developments.headline: Church demolished after two decades vacant
developments.detail: Demolition crews razed the 127-year-old St. George's Church on Washington Street to make way for affordable housing. Former parishioner Joan Graciale, married there in 1980 with all her children baptized there, walked from her home to ask the crew for bricks as mementos; some stained glass remained unbroken as the last walls came down.

sources.title: Haverhill says goodbye to St. George's Church; beloved building is torn down
Saying goodbye to St. George's Church

developments.headline: Seven affordable homes completed on church site
developments.detail: Governor Charlie Baker, Lt. Governor Karyn Polito, and state housing officials visited Haverhill to celebrate seven new affordable homes — three duplexes and one single-family house — built by Bread & Roses Housing on the site of the former St. George's Church on Washington Street.

sources.title: Baker celebrates new Bread & Roses housing in Haverhill

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: Three refinements to the archive record. (1) The parish is not unnamed: it was St. George's (Šv. Jurgio), confirmed as Haverhill's Lithuanian parish by Merrimack Valley Life ("St. George's for the Lithuanians" in the 1998 four-parish consolidation into All Saints) and matching Global True Lithuania's Haverhill Lithuanian church (built 1892, closed 1998, demolished 2019 — the Eagle-Tribune's "127-year-old building" in 2019 confirms the 1892 build). (2) "Razed by 2023" is imprecise: demolition occurred in mid-November 2019, documented in real time by the Eagle-Tribune and Haverhill Gazette. (3) "Only a cornerstone survives" could not be corroborated — the site has since been fully redeveloped with seven homes; press documented a parishioner salvaging loose bricks during demolition but no cornerstone preservation. "Abandoned by 2017" is consistent but understated: the building had been unoccupied since roughly the 1998 closure (press: "two decades of sitting unoccupied"), after a stint as a police precinct. Ending as diocese_closed is confirmed: the Archdiocese of Boston merged it into All Saints Parish in 1998.
gaps: No published sale price or transaction date for the property's transfer from the Archdiocese of Boston to Bread & Roses Housing was found; the archdiocese-to-nonprofit chain of title is inferred from the nonprofit developing the "former St. George's Church" site but the deed terms were not located. The exact street number on Washington Street was not published in the articles opened. The parish's founding date (Lithuanians reportedly acquired the 1892 building around 1910 per Global True Lithuania, an undated community-maintained source) is not confirmed by primary records. The archive's claim that a cornerstone survives could not be verified or refuted — no source opened mentions the cornerstone's fate. Searches for Lithuanian community reunions, memorials, or preservation activity for this parish in 2023-2026 returned nothing (results were dominated by the unrelated St. George Lithuanian parishes in Shenandoah, PA and Rochester, NY). Both 2019 Gazette/Eagle-Tribune article bodies are partially paywalled; facts were taken from their accessible ledes, photo captions, and metadata.
